text: The surgeon opens the left posterior rectus sheath, cutting approximately one centimeter from the linea alba, revealing the preperitoneal dissection in the midline behind the diastatic linea alba. The dissection proceeds towards the pelvis, cutting through the arcuate line and transversalis fascia, addressing smaller hernias around the umbilicus. The surgeon is anterior to the medial umbilical ligaments, which are divided, and enters the hernia sac in the right lower quadrant, providing a view into the hernia.

The surgeon begins dissection around the caudal end of the hernia, dividing the hernia sac at the neck due to difficulty in preserving it. The pseudosac is recruited to patch a posterior defect, allowing closure without bilateral tar. Dissection occurs in the subcutaneous tissue and the right groin, removing peritoneum from the vas deferens and testicular vessels. The preperitoneal fat and fascia are opened to reveal the transversus abdominis, creating a preperitoneal tunnel by pulling fat off the transversalis fascia. The surgeon lyses a colon stuck within the lateral part of the hernia sac and removes polypropylene from the old mesh inside the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon enters the right retrorectus space, opening the right posterior rectus sheath to create a 15 cm cephalad overlap. The procedure involves performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR) to extend laterally from the hernia defect. The surgeon carefully preserves a large medial neurovascular bundle by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and lifting the nerve off the transversus abdominis. The TAR is extended to the cephalad edge of the hernia defect, transitioning into the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon evaluates the junction between the pre-transversalis and pre-peritoneal spaces in the upper abdomen, preferring the pre-transversalis space for dissection. The pre-peritoneal fat is dissected off the transversalis fascia, revealing a vascular plane. The surgeon scores the edge of the hernia defect laterally, transitioning between the pre-peritoneal and pre-transversalis spaces. The dissection continues into the groin, staying external to the pre-peritoneal fascia and fat.

The surgeon cuts the preperitoneal fascia and fat to combine spaces, revealing a large abdomen with a defect measuring 13 cm tall and 20 cm wide. A shrunken Bard Ventral-X composite mesh is visible within the hernia sac, characterized by its firm texture. The mesh is excised and set aside for later extraction, while the surgeon prepares to close the posterior layer.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to re-approximate the tissue, closing a large posterior defect between the right-sided lower TAR and the recruited pseudo-sac. The stretchy pseudo-sac material aids in the closure process.

The surgeon uses image inversion to facilitate suturing of a large defect, employing a technique of placing multiple sutures without fully tightening them to distribute tension evenly. The defect is gradually closed, resembling a corset, under pneumoperitoneum at eight millimeters mercury. A small gap is left for mesh extraction through a four-centimeter incision at the old scar, after which the defect closure is completed robotically.

The surgeon inserts a 37 cm by 30 cm medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh into the abdominal cavity. The mesh is unfurled from the patient's right side to the left side, with the ventral surface positioned against the groin and lateral abdominal wall. It is secured with 3-0 Vicryl sutures to prevent lateral shifting. The mesh is laid to conform to the lateral abdominal wall and pelvic side wall, with the final suture placed near Cooper's ligament. As the abdomen is desufflated, the mesh is pushed against the abdominal and pelvic walls, ensuring coverage of the inguinal and flank regions. A 19 French round JP drain is placed through the left lower quadrant port site.
